coredata.dc:title: Bioactive carbon dots lights up microtubules and destabilises cell cytoskeletal framework – A robust imaging agent with therapeutic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, S. Uday
Bhushan, Bharat
Gopinath, P.
coredata.dc:description: A class of bioactive, microtubule specific, self-targeted carbon dots (CDs) has been synthesised by simple one-step hydrothermal treatment with Catharanthus roseus as the precursor. Apart from excitation dependent multicolour fluorescence of as-prepared CDs, they were also attributed with an inherent affinity for microtubules of cell cytoskeletal framework. As prepared CDs in-vitro bio-labelling and live cell imaging studies in NIH 3T3 cells illustrates their tubulin specific bio-labelling potential which lights up the cell microtubule framework prominently under all filters. The ability of as-prepared CDs to simultaneously label tubulin and effectuate microtubule depolymerisation and fragmentation enables us to track its therapeutic potential on a real time basis. The cascade of events starting from cellular uptake, microtubule labelling and subsequent cytoskeletal changes (microtubule fragmentation, depolymerisation and cytoplasmic constriction) have been investigated comprehensively in this work by flow cytometer, confocal microscope, AFM microscopy (Peak Force QNM) and FE-SEM analysis. In summary, this work outlines the synthesis, characterization and application of a new class of highly fluorescent self-targeted microtubule specific CDs as a potent bioactive imaging nanotags.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanotags
Bioactive imaging
Bio-imaging
Multifunctional nanomaterials

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: AFM analysis of cell adhesion modulation by CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of the formation process of CDs from C. roseus leaves by hydrothermal treatment. (b) TEM (c) FE-SEM images of CDs. Inset: size distribution histogram of CDs. (d) Elemental mapping of CDs. Individual elemental distribution (red for carbon, yellow for nitrogen, green for oxygen, purple for sulphur and blue for phosphorous). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) XRD pattern, (b) FTIR spectrum, (c) TG analysis and (d) DLS spectrum showing the size distribution of aqueous suspension of CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320nm to 480nm with increments of 20nm. (c) Fluorescence decay curve of CDs and (d) Dependence of fluorescence emission intensity against time depicting photostability of CDs (λex =320nm; λem =400nm).

(a) Plot of normalized emission intensity versus different ionic strength for CDs (b) pH-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (c) Changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs in different solvents (F/F0 denotes the ratio of fluorescence intensity of CDs in different organic solvents vs. that in water) (d) Cell detachment assay after incubation with different concentrations of CDs. (error bars denote SD; n=3).

Flow cytometric analysis of cellular uptake of CDs in NIH 3T3 cells treated with different concentration of CDs (a) untreated and (b) 100μgmL−1 (c) 200μgmL−1 (d) 300μgmL−1 (e) 400μgmL−1 (f) 500μgmL−1.

Confocal microscopy images of fluorescent CDs labelled NIH 3T3 cells under (a, e) red filter, (b, f) green filter and (c, g) DAPI filter, (d, h) overlay of images acquired under all three filters. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal microscopy images of fluorescent CDs labelled NIH 3T3 cells under (a, d) red filter, (b, e) green filter and (c, f) DAPI filter indicating cytoskeletal reorganization dynamics. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effect of CDs on cell cycle distribution profiles in NIH 3T3 cells was evaluated by flow cytometric analysis of PI-stained samples. (a) Untreated and (b) CDs treated cells; (c) Modulus map of NIH 3T3 treated with bioactive CDs and its correlation with (d) topographical of cell as acquired by PeakForce QNM studies. (e) Topographical line map along three different segments located at the cell periphery. (f) Higher magnification topographical map of adhesion junctions of bipolar NIH 3T3 after CDs treatment.

head.ce:title: Bioactive carbon dots lights up microtubules and destabilises cell cytoskeletal framework – A robust imaging agent with therapeutic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanobiotechnology Laboratory, Centre for Nanotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Roorkee, Uttarakhand, 247667, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Roorkee, Uttarakhand, 247667, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Nanobiotechnology Laboratory, Centre for Nanotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Roorkee, Uttarakhand, 247667, India.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A class of bioactive, microtubule specific, self-targeted carbon dots (CDs) has been synthesised by simple one-step hydrothermal treatment with Catharanthus roseus as the precursor. Apart from excitation dependent multicolour fluorescence of as-prepared CDs, they were also attributed with an inherent affinity for microtubules of cell cytoskeletal framework. As prepared CDs in-vitro bio-labelling and live cell imaging studies in NIH 3T3 cells illustrates their tubulin specific bio-labelling potential which lights up the cell microtubule framework prominently under all filters. The ability of as-prepared CDs to simultaneously label tubulin and effectuate microtubule depolymerisation and fragmentation enables us to track its therapeutic potential on a real time basis. The cascade of events starting from cellular uptake, microtubule labelling and subsequent cytoskeletal changes (microtubule fragmentation, depolymerisation and cytoplasmic constriction) have been investigated comprehensively in this work by flow cytometer, confocal microscope, AFM microscopy (Peak Force QNM) and FE-SEM analysis. In summary, this work outlines the synthesis, characterization and application of a new class of highly fluorescent self-targeted microtubule specific CDs as a potent bioactive imaging nanotags.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with size ranging from several atoms to few nanometre have been widely used in various biomedical applications, particularly in bio-imaging, therapeutics and sensing, because of their low cost, easy surface modification, biocompatibility, abundance in nature, high hydrophilicity, chemical and photochemical stability and high quantum yield [1–3]. Thus, they emerged as a legitimate competitor to the conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) with comparable or even better performance in the context of biological applications by overcoming the various drawbacks associated with them such as heavy metal toxicity, expensive precursor and poor photostability [4,5]. Recent research has been focused on the development of multifunctional fluorescent CDs that can be exploited for bio-imaging along with antioxidant [6], sensing [7,8], photo catalytic and therapeutic properties [9–12]. So far, various techniques have been employed for the synthesis of CDs [13–16], among them hydrothermal methods have gain significant interest among researchers from the perspective of cheap, eco-friendly and green approach without the involvement of any surface passivation agents [17–20]. Another major aspect in the preparation of CDs is the selection of the appropriate precursor source, which plays a significant role in determining the physical and biological properties of the CDs. Recently, a variety of natural renewable products including coffee, green tea, garlic, ginger, etc have been exploited for the preparation of CDs due to their inherent therapeutic properties, inexpensive and abundance in nature [6,9,17]. The therapeutic potential of these CDs has been demonstrated in a surfeit of cell lines and animal models.

Catharanthus roseus L. (apocyanaceae) an annual plant known with several names (such as Madagascar periwinkle, Vincarosea, Lochnera rosea, etc.) is native to the Caribbean Basin and has been widely used traditional medicinal plant all over the world, including India to treat a wide assortment of diseases [21]. Indian herbalists and Ayurvedic physicians used the water decoction of the leaves, root and/or the whole plant for conditions as varied as cancer, Hodgkin’s disease, menorrhagia, diabetes, wound healing and numerous skin problems such as acne, eczema, dermatitis, etc [21,22]. There are more than 400 known alkaloids including vincristine, vinblastine, vincamine, vinorelbine and vindesine isolated from periwinkle. Some of these compounds are approved for their antineoplastic activities to treat malignant lymphomas, neuroblastoma, leukemia, rhabdomyosarcoma, Wilm’s tumor, Hodgkin’s disease and several other cancers [21–23]. Apart from that its memory-enhancing and vasodilating properties has been also exploited for vascular dementia and Alzheimer's disease [24,25].

This study aims to develop highly water-soluble multicolour fluorescent CDs with inherent microtubule specific bioactivity from Catharanthus roseus leaves through a single step, green approach without utilizing any surface passivating agents. To the best of our knowledge, there is no previous literature report on “C. roseus derived fluorescent carbon dots”. In this study, we report for the first time, the effect of bioactive fluorescent carbon dots (without any modification) on dynamic stability of microtubules in fibroblast (NIH 3T3) cells.

The leaves were plucked from rosy periwinkle plants present in the departmental garden and washed thoroughly with distilled water before use. All the chemicals used were of analytical grade and were used without any further purification or modification. Quinine sulphate was procured from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd, India. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and nutrient broth (NB) were purchased from Himedia. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), hexane, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), isopropanol were supplied by Rankem Pvt. Ltd, India; dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), diethyl ether were procured from SD-Fine Chemicals Limited (SDFCL), India, while ethylene glycol, dichloromethane (DCM), sodium chloride (NaCl), and acetone were supplied by Sisco Laboratories Pvt. Ltd (SRL), India. Chloroform and tert-butanol were procured from Merck (Germany). Tetrahydrofuran, and glutaraldehyde were purchased from Sigma. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol- 2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) dye, methanol was procured from Amresco, USA. All the solutions were prepared in Milli-Q water (Millipore).

The bioactive, multicolour fluorescent CDs described in this work were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of Rosy Periwinkle (Catharanthus roseus) leaves. In brief, 10g of finely chopped fresh rosy periwinkle leaves and 60mL of Milli-Q water was added into a hydrothermal vessel and heated at 200°C for 10h. After the hydrothermal treatment the resulting black carbonized solution was allowed to gradually cool down to room temperature. The resultant product was then centrifuged at 28,672xg for 15min to remove the large insoluble particles. The supernatant solution was filtered through 0.22μm filter membrane and stored at room temperature, which were then used as and when required for subsequent characterization and invitro experiments.

Fluorescence measurements were carried out by using Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan) and Cytation 3 cell imaging multi-mode microplate reader (Biotek), while absorption and fluorescent decay studies were conducted by UV–vis double beam spectrophotometer (Lasany, LI-2800) and Horiba JobinYvon “FluoroCube Fluorescence Lifetime System” equipped with a Nano LED (635nm) source, respectively. The fluorescence decay curve analysis and life time calculation were carried out by using IBH decay analysis v 6.1 software. The functional groups present in as prepared CDs were characterised by acquiring FTIR spectra on Thermo Nicolet spectrometer in the range 4000–400cm−1 using KBr pellets. Morphological analysis of the CDs treated NIH 3T3 cells and particle size analysis of CDs were carried by FE-SEM (Carl Zeiss ULTRA PLUS) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray detector (EDX) operating at an accelerating voltage of 15–20keV) and TEM (FEI TECHNAI G2 20 S-TWIN) operating at 200keV. The particle size was further estimated by processing the acquired images by using Image J software. The average hydrodynamic diameter of CDs was estimated by using Malvern, Nano ZS 90 zetasizer. XRD analysis was carried out by Bruker AXS D8 Advance powder X-ray diffractometer (Cu-Kα radiation, λ=1.5406A°) at a scan speed of 1°/min in the range of 10–90∘. The thermal stability analysis of CDs was estimated by EXSTAR TG/DTA 6300 thermal analyzer from 30°C to 800°C under controlled nitrogen atmosphere with constant heating rate of 10°C/min. The morphological changes in the cells and biolabelling potential of CDs were analysed through confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss) system with laser excitations and filters corresponding to CY3, GFP457, and DAPI.

The quantum yield of the synthesized CDs was measured by using quinine sulphate as standard having quantum yield of 0.54 (in 0.1M H2SO4) at 360nm as reported earlier [9,16]. The fluorescence quantum yield of as-prepared CDs was evaluated according to the following equation:
Q
=
Q
R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η
21
η
2 R
Where Q refers to the quantum yield of synthesized CDs, I denotes calculated integrated emission intensity (area under the curve), whereas η and A represents the refractive index and the optical density. The subscript R is assigned to the standard fluorophore i.e. quinine sulphate. The absorption value was always kept under 0.1 in order to avoid the chance of any reabsorption effect.

The stability of emission was estimated by continuous excitation of the samples kept in the 96 well black plate at 25°C inside a multimode plate reader (Biotek) equipped with Xenon flash lamp. The samples were excited at 320nm and their corresponding emission was monitored at 390nm with a time scan range of 9000s (2h 30min).

NIH 3T3 (mouse embryonic fibroblast) cells were obtained from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India and maintained in DMEM medium supplemented with 1% (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin solution and 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 5% CO2 in a humidified incubator at 37°C

The effect of CDs on the inhibition of microtubules formation was investigated indirectly by estimating the detached cells via MTT assay. In brief, 104 cells per well were seeded in 96 well plate and allow to adhere. After overnight incubation, the cells were treated with desired concentrations of CDs for 24h. Thereafter, the spent medium was removed and the detached cells were removed completely by brief PBS wash, followed by incubation with 100μL of fresh medium containing 10μL MTT (5mgmL−1) at 37°C for 4h. During the incubation period, the MTT reagent was converted into an insoluble formazan crystals by the mitochondrial activity of live adhere cells. The formed formazan crystal were then dissolved by adding 100μL of solubilization solution. Finally, after 20min of shaking over a gyratory shaker, absorbance of each well was recorded at 570nm using multimode plate reader (Cytation 3, Biotek).

To monitor the effect of CDs on cell proliferation, cell cycle analysis was performed by staining the cells with PI and subsequent analysis by flow cytometry. Around 1×105 NIH 3T3 cells were seeded in 6 well plate and after overnight attachment the cells were treated with CDs 500μgmL−1 for 10h. At the end of treatment time span, cells were briefly rinsed with PBS and subsequently trypsinized and centrifuged (500Xg for 5min) to obtain the cell pellet. The resultant cell pellet was resuspended in 70% ethanol fixative and kept in ice for 10min. The cells were then treated with 50μgmL−1 PI, 1mg/ml RNase A and 0.05% TRITON X-100 and incubated in dark for 30min at 37°C [26]. The cells were then analyzed by flow cytometer (Amnis Flowsight) for 10,000 events and processed further by IDEAS software to represent the results as histograms.

In order to investigate the biolabelling potential of CDs along with their ability to effect microtubules, NIH 3T3 cells were cultured over coverslip and then incubated in DMEM medium containing 250μgmL−1 CDs for 12h. At the end of the incubation period, the spent medium was removed carefully and the cells were then fixed with 2% glutaraldehyde solution for 5min. After glutaraldehyde treatment, cells were briefly rinsed with ethanol gradient solution (i.e. 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%) and allowed to air dry for a while. The fixed cells were then mounted onto glass slide with DPX solution and examined by confocal microscopes under blue, green and DAPI filters.

In order to study the time dependent effect of CDs on cell morphology, NIH 3T3 cells were treated with 200μg/ml CDs and monitored by JULI Smart fluorescent cell analyzer inside CO2 incubator at 37°C. The NIH 3T3 cells were incubated with 200μg/ml CDs for 3h after which cells were subsequently replenished with fresh media and images were captured under green filter at interval of 30min for a duration of 16h. At the end of 16h, all the captured images were merged together to generate time lapse video of NIH 3T3 cells response to CDs treatment.

In order to evaluate the uptake of CDs by NIH 3T3 cells, 2×105 fibroblast cells were seeded per well in a 6-well plate and allowed to adhere overnight. Thereafter, the cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs in DMEM medium for 12h. After incubation, the spent medium was discarded and cells were trypsinized and pooled together by centrifugation at 500×g for 5min. The resulting cell pellet was then resuspended in PBS and carried over for quantitative cell associated fluorescence assessment by flow cytometer analysis (Amnis Flowsight). Around 104 cells were monitored for each sample and acquired cell population fluorescence was represented as histogram with random gating of five successive regions of relatively higher fluorescence. The population gating and subsequent data analysis was carried out by Amnis IDEAS software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFM analysis of cell adhesion modulation by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of CDs on spatiotemporal changes in the nanomechanical property of NIH 3T3 cells were analysed by PeakForce QNM technique in Dimension Icon AFM (Bruker). ScanAsyst-Fluid probe (with spring constant of 0.5N/m) was used in peak force tapping mode to generate force curve for each contact it makes with the samples surface which were subsequently resolved to determine quantitative nanomechanical properties like height, adhesion, dissipation, DMT modulus, deformation and peak force error. Young’s modulus at each point was determined using DMT (Derjaguin–Mueller–Toporov) fit with the force curve. The probe was maintained at ramp rate of 1 Htz and 50μm scan size divided into 514×514 was adapted for analysis. Throughout the study, NIH 3T3 cells were maintained inside perfusing stage incubator (PSI) chamber integrated to Dimension Icon AFM microscope for mapping of nanomechanical property to different cell features.

C. roseus is a traditional medicinal plant commonly used around the world owing to their medicinal properties. The CDs has been prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of C. roseus leaves (Fig. 1 (a)), which undergoes carbonization, dehydration, polymerization and passivation, resulted in the formation of pale yellow colour solution of fluorescent CDs. [6,9,27]. The representative TEM images of as-prepared CDs (Fig. 1(b)) clearly depicts that the CDs were uniform, monodispersed spheres with relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 1.8 to 3.2nm. The histogram resulting from statistical size distribution analysis reveals that the CDs have an average mean diameter of around 2.51±0.31nm (Fig. 1(b)). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy analysis (EDX) of as-prepared CDs indicated prominent presence of C, O, N elements whereas S and P were sparsely distributed (Fig. 1(c&d)). Furthermore, DLS results revealed average hydrodynamic diameter of CDs as 8.75nm (Fig. 2 (d)), which clearly correlates with the TEM observation and also suggests that the CDs were stable in aqueous biological environment and did not undergo aggregation.

The XRD pattern of CDs shown in Fig. 2 (a) display a typical single broad peak at around 22°, which is consistent with that of previously reported (002) plane of carbon for CDs. Thus, confirms the amorphous nature of CDs [9]. In order to investigate the chemical composition of as-prepared CDs FTIR spectrum was recorded (Fig. 2(b)). A broad absorption band at 3406.88cm−1 was detected, corresponds to the overlapping OH/NH stretching bands [28], while peak at 2937.62cm−1 was assigned to CH stretching vibrations [29], whereas NH deformation vibration was found at 1594.55cm−1 [16,29–31]. In addition, the absorption peaks at 1664.04cm−1 and 1402.77cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibration of CO, CN bonds, respectively. While peaks at 1117.38cm−1 and 1076.83cm−1 allocated to the stretching and bending vibrations of CO and COC bonds. Apart from it, the peak at 1310.61cm−1 represented asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of COC, while the absorption peak at 678.14cm−1 is assigned to the C−S group [6,16,29–31]. Thus, the above results suggested that the as-prepared CDs were functionalized with hydroxyl, amino, sulphur, carboxylic, alkyl and aryl groups derived from organic moieties in C. roseus leaves under hydrothermal treatment. As a results of these functional groups CDs exhibits exceptional water solubility and remained stable over a period of time without undergoing aggregations. The thermal stability of CDs was analysed by thermogravimetric (TG) analysis. Fig. 2(c) clearly shows an initial weight loss of 13.49% at 100°C due to the removal of moisture and water molecules associated with CDs. Thereafter, a stepwise degradation of CDs was observed corresponds to the gradual loss of surface functional groups. Around 500°C more than 80% of weight loss was observed, beyond which no significant weight loss was noticed as a result of char formation in nitrogen atmosphere due to which the rate of CDs degradation become extremely slow [29,31–33].

Fig. 3 (a) depicts the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared CDs similar to the previously reported CDs obtained from the natural sources in the range from 200 to 600nm [6,34]. The absorption spectrum indicates a broad peak around 270nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra show a typical excitation dependent emission behaviour of CDs as illustrated in Fig. 3(b). On increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 480, a concurrent decrease in emission intensity was noticed along with the shifting of maximum emission from 390 to 510nm. Such shifts in the emission peak is suggested due to the presence of different emissive sites on the surfaces of the CDs along with the existence of varied size of CDs, responsible for the multicolour fluorescence of CDs [1,2,14,35]. However, the exact mechanism for the excitation wavelength dependence of emission wavelengths needs to be established. The fluorescent quantum yield was measured by using quinine sulphate as standard and found to be 3.39%. Moreover, the fluorescent lifetime decay curve of as-prepared CDs can be very well fitted to a triple-exponential function as shown in Fig. 3(c) and the mean fluorescence lifetime of CDs was computed to be 5.59ns (Table S1), such a short lifetime represents that the radiative recombination nature of excitations is the possible luminescent mechanism [34].

The as-prepared CDs exhibits fairly good photostability, the PL intensity decreased marginally (7.17%) under continuous illumination for 2h 30min (Fig. 3(d)). Thus, the results support the bio-imaging potential of CDs. In order to evaluate the stability of CDs under high ionic strength environments, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was monitored in different concentration of NaCl solution. As illustrated in Fig. 4 (a), no significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs were noticed even at higher concentration of NaCl (1M), suggesting the stability of CDs in high ionic strength medium. The effect of solvents on the fluorescence intensity and solubility of CDs were also examined (Fig. 4 (c)). The results illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was only marginally different in water and other alcoholic solvents suggesting the existence of hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs. Moreover, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed in ethylene glycol solution, followed by water and other alcohols. However, in other organic solvents the solubility of CDs was significantly reduced, which leads to the decrease in the fluorescence intensity. In order to investigate the applicability of CDs as fluorescent biomarker under physiological conditions, the fluorescent stability of as-prepared CDs was monitored under different condition. The effect of pH on the fluorescence property of CDs was analysed by adjusting the pH with 0.1N solutions of HCl and NaOH under constant ionic strengths conditions in the range from pH 3–11 as shown in Fig. 4 (b). The fluorescence intensity decrease with increase in pH from 4 to 11. However, under extreme acidic condition (pH=3) a sudden decrease in the fluorescence intensity was noticed corresponds to the decrease in the colloidal stability of CDs.

In order to understand the effect of CDs on the inhibition of microtubules formation and their effect on the attachment of cells to the surface MTT assay was performed. Fig. 4 (d) shows that the CDs significantly induced the dose dependent microtubule inhibition that leads to the detachment of fibroblast cells. Up to 200μgmL−1 concentration no significant effect was observed, beyond which the cell detached in a concentration dependent manner. Around 290μgmL−1 concentration half of the cells population get detached. Previous studies have shown that the alkaloid isolated from C. roseus leaves significantly target and bind to the microtubules [36], thus our study supports that the as-prepared CDs specifically binds to the microtubule.

The CDs cellular uptake studies was investigated in NIH 3T3 at different concentrations by flow cytometer. It was observed that with increase in concentration of CDs, there was proportional increase in cell associated fluorescence which clearly indicates higher cellular uptake and intracellular localization of CDs. The histogram plot was randomly gated into different sections to portray shift in cell fluorescence with each treatment. At 100μgmL−1 CDs concentration, the cells fluorescence was distinctly higher and the cell population was prominently distributed under R3 and R4 (i.e. 33.9% and 64% respectively) and was well resolved and much ahead of untreated control cells (gated under region R1) (Fig. 5 ). With subsequent increase in CDs concentration the cell population distribution gradually shifted towards higher fluorescence for instance at 400μgmL−1 CDs the most of the cell population shifted to R4 and R5 region (i.e. 65.8%, 27% and 7.25% respectively) and were completely excluded from R3 region. Although at 500μgmL−1 the increase in cell fluorescence was not as large as observed between lower concentrations which indicates that cell associated fluorescence begins to saturate after certain concentration (i.e. after 500μgmL−1 CDs concentration). Moreover, affinity of these CDs for microtubule facilitates their retention within cells for prolonged duration and thereby enables us to monitor ensuing cytoskeletal changes. It would be further illustrated in subsequent sections that at lower concentration these CDs merely suppress microtubule dynamics whereas at higher concentrations they depolymerize microtubule and reduce their polymer mass. Thus, as apparent from cellular uptake studies as-prepared CDs effectively label cells both a lower and higher concentrations enabling us to simultaneously manifest and monitor cytoskeletal changes at diverse therapeutic dosages.

After uptake of these bioactive CDs, cells undergo gross morphological changes with the passage of time. Fluorescent images of NIH 3T3 cells treated with CDs (Fig. 6 (a–h)) clearly indicate internalization of CDs and its subsequent sequestration onto microtubule framework and thus rendering them distinctly visible under all filters. As indicated in Fig. 6(e–h), these fluorescent CDs apart from labeling microtubules also lead to swift de-polymerization of microtubules, which subsequently destabilizes the cell cytoskeleton framework. Initially these CDs act upon microtubules to form dense filamentous aggregates and gradually disassemble them to give raise to discontinuous and fragmented microtubule segments throughout the cytoplasm [37]. The free microtubule ends that arise out of microtubule de-polymerization subsequently aggregate together to form prominent foci throughout the cell cytoskeletal network. The smaller fragmented and depolymerized fragments of microtubule remain labelled with CDs and begin to accumulate within the cells.

As depicted in Fig. 7 (c&f), cell periphery begins to slack and lose its adhesion junctions along the direction of microtubule de-polymerization and subsequently adapts a bipolar dendritic shape. Apart from this, during the course of cell retraction, cells also begin to lose intercellular adhesion junction (like desmosomes, tight junctions and gap junctions) which further leads to contact inhibition of cell proliferation [38]. The Fig. 7(a–f) illustrates that, in presence of intact and labelled microtubules (along the direction of two arrows) cell holds its periphery and retain its shape, whereas the triangular portion spanning in-between two arrows lacks microtubules as a consequence prominent slack (curved periphery) is introduced in the cell periphery along its direction. Thus, bioactive CDs apart from inflicting its microtubule depolymerizing activity also enables us to simultaneously track their mode of action due to its inherent fluorescence.

Another distinctive feature of these fluorescent bioactive CDs was that, although they labelled complete microtubules framework of the cells, the polymerized microtubules were prominently seen in green and red filter whereas depolymerized microtubule fragments were distinctly visible in blue filter alone (Fig. 6(g)). Such microtubule conformation dependent fluorescence of CDs can further be exploited effectively in tracking their therapeutic efficacy with respect to different dosage levels on a real time basis.

Time lapse video of NIH 3T3 cells treated with bioactive CDs illustrates the cascade of morphological changes that cell undergo with respect to time (Vid. 1). During the initial 3h of incubation itself, cells take up sufficient CDs to be distinctly visible under green filter thereafter. In spite of removing exogenous CDs after three hours, the cells still retained their fluorescence throughout the ensuing study (i.e. for 16h) which clearly indicates strong affinity of CDs for cytoskeletal element, microtubule. Apart from this, overall cell fluorescence gradually begins to increase with progressive constriction of cells, which again reinstates the fact that internalized CDs are retained as such within the cells for a prolonged duration. With the passage of time, internalized CDs leads to swift retraction of cell periphery and consequently constricts the cells to adapt dendritic structure. The cells undergo progressive de-polymerization of microtubules followed by aggregation of fragmented microtubules and foci formation throughout the cytoplasm. Microtubule de-polymerization by CDs also generates large lacuna in the cytoplasm that is devoid of microtubule. Such lacunae subsequently increase in size with respect to time as CDs subsequently dismantles the cell cytoskeleton.

The as-prepared CDs with an inherent ability to breakdown cell microtubule framework, is certainly expected to arrest cell division. Thus, in order to monitor the effect of CDs on cell proliferation, cycle analysis was carried out. It was observed that upon treating cells with CDs the cells were predominantly arrested in G2/M phase (29.8%) as compared to untreated cells (10.9%) as shown in Fig. 8 (a&b). Such observation clearly indicates that CDs microtubule inhibiting activity arrests chromosomal segregation during nuclear division and thereby arrests cells in G2/M phase. Alongside drastic surge in G2/M population upon CDs treatment, equivalent decline in G0/G1 cell population (50.4% compared to 74.9% of untreated cells) was also observed which ascertains that although the cells have completed DNA synthesis (S phase) they could not proceed through subsequent events of karyokinesis due to CDs mediated destabilization of cell microtubules.

A high resolution map of surface topography and young’s modulus of NIH 3T3 cells treated with 300μgmL−1 CDs is depicted in Fig. 8(c)&(d). The topographical image of one end of bipolar dendritic NIH 3T3 cell closely portrays the cascade of morphological changes that cells manifest upon treatment with CDs. The presence of traces of detached collagen fibrils alongside cell periphery ascertains the event and course of cell retraction. Apart from this, an abrupt increase in height at the cell boundary was also observed which is an outcome of aggregation of de-polymerized microtubule fragments arising from the retracting end. Such event of microtubule aggregation at the cell periphery was also tracked in live cell imaging experiment where it led to progressive increase in fluorescence throughout the cell boundary. Alongside microtubules, another major component of cell cytoskeleton framework i.e microfilaments (made up of actin fibers) although not acted upon by CDs also undergo drastic reorganization. Along the retracting ends, actin stress fibers are synthesised to reinforce the retracting cells for a brief period which is evident in topographical map as linear straight segments holding the cell periphery (Fig. 8(e)) [39]. Higher magnification images of cell terminals revealed presence of CDs aggregates tracing all along the cell boundary (Fig. 8(d)). In addition to this, topographical map also indicated presence of slacked microtubules which ascertains the events of microtubule depolymerisation and fragmentation. As indicated in Fig. 8(f), these fragments subsequently aggregate to form large, elevated and disorganised microtubule clusters called ‘foci’. Topography line map (line A, B and C) across different sections of cell periphery clearly depicts the sudden surge in height as a result of accumulation of microtubule aggregates and presence of actin stress fibers along the retracting end. These actin stress fibers at the boundary were mapped to regions of higher stiffness as compared to rest of the cell. Stiffness map clearly embosses traces of collagen fibrils emanating from the cells boundary as they are associated with higher stiffness value. The centre portion of cell body away from the periphery consist of numerous slacked microtubules present as foci and thus corresponds to lower modulus values indicating a destabilised cytoskeletal framework of CDs treated cells. Thus AFM analysis elucidates events of swift cytoskeletal remodelling and change in cell morphology when NIH 3T3 cells were treated with CDs.

Similar observation were also made by FE-SEM analysis of CDs treated NIH 3T3 cells, wherein the retracting ends of cells were further magnified to illustrate surface localised CDs at the periphery of cells (Fig. S1). The irregular morphology and abrupt differences in cell topography clearly indicates loss in stiffness of cell cytoskeletal framework which arise due to microtubule depolymerising activity of as-synthesised CDs [40]. All along the cell periphery the CDs aggregated due to subsequent constriction of cells which, if remains unabated would lead to complete detachment of cells.

Carbon dots synthesized in this work are derived from a source rich in vinca alkaloids. These vinca alkaloids are already well known for their tubulin specific binding affinity [41]. They tend to bind rapidly and reversibly to specific domains of the microtubule and interrupt microtubule congregation. Preexisting literature clearly illustrate that these vinca alkaloids have two binding sites per mole of tubulin dimer which can even vary to 16–17 high-affinity binding sites at the ends of each microtubule [42]. Upon binding, they mediate a conformational change in the tubule domain involved in it self-association. They often exhibit preferential binding onto microtubule plus end to reduce both treadmilling and dynamic stability by ∼50%, and thereby mediate microtubule depolymerization [43]. The fluorescent microscopy results clearly indicate that the C-dots were attached to the microtubules and only the fluorescent labelled microtubules were undergoing depolymerization which further confirms their microtubule destabilization activity.

In present study, we have described a facile, eco-friendly, economically feasible and green synthetic approach for synthesizing intrinsically multicolour, fluorescent CDs by hydrothermal reactions with C. roseus leaves as herbal precursor. The as-prepared CDs exhibits excitation dependent multicolour fluorescence with high photostability even in aqueous solution. The versatility of these CDs for diverse applications was further established by monitoring effect of ionic concentration, solvent and pH on fluorescence of CDs. The potential application of as-prepared CDs as bio-labelling agents was investigated against NIH 3T3 cells which illustrated that these CDs apart from effectively labelling cells (under all three filters) also exhibited inherent tubulin specific bioactivity. These CDs where observed to orchestrate microtubule fragmentation, depolymerisation in NIH 3T3 cells which subsequently lead to extensive destabilization of cytoskeletal framework and cytoplasmic constriction. The cascade of cytoskeletal changes and its effect on cell morphology and proliferation have been investigated comprehensively in the present work by confocal microscopy, FE-SEM, AFM (Peakforce QNM studies) and flow cytometer. In brief, the work outlines synthesis and application of first ever multicolour fluorescent CDs with inherent microtubule specific bioactivity and further elucidates its scope for efficient bioactive imaging applications.

SUK and BB are thankful to the Ministry of Human Resource Development, Government of India, for the fellowship. Sincere thanks to Department of Chemical Engineering and Institute Instrumentation Centre, IIT Roorkee, for the various analytical facilities provided.
